introSection: River King Mine Train is a steel hybrid terrain roller coaster located at Six Flags St. Louis in Eureka, Missouri, USA.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The coaster was one of two similar coasters placed side-by-side that together formed the River King Mine Train, an opening year attraction at Six Flags Over Mid-America. One track opened with the park on June 5, 1971 while the second opened on August 10, bringing the reported capacity to 5,000 riders per hour.

In 1984, the attraction received modifications, including stand-up trains and would be renamed Rail Blazer. On July 7 of the same year, a 46-year-old Indianapolis woman was killed after falling from the ride. Park officials claimed that the woman fainted and fell out of the car, but her husband, who had been beside her, said that she had not fainted but had simply been tossed from the ride when it whipped around a curve. There were also several issues with the track and trains. For the 1985 season, Six Flags recalled the stand-up trains and restored the original River King Mine Train name.

After the 1987 season, one track was left standing but not operating. It was removed in 1988 and sold, being relocated to Dollywood where opened as Thunder Express in 1989.

The ride's height requirement was increased from to  in 2025.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Black track with unpainted wooden supports.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On October 4, 2025, a 16-year-old employee reportedly fell approximately from the ride's platform.

fullText:
River King Mine Train is a steel hybrid terrain roller coaster located at Six Flags St. Louis in Eureka, Missouri, USA.

The coaster was one of two similar coasters placed side-by-side that together formed the River King Mine Train, an opening year attraction at Six Flags Over Mid-America. One track opened with the park on June 5, 1971 while the second opened on August 10, bringing the reported capacity to 5,000 riders per hour.

In 1984, the attraction received modifications, including stand-up trains and would be renamed Rail Blazer. On July 7 of the same year, a 46-year-old Indianapolis woman was killed after falling from the ride. Park officials claimed that the woman fainted and fell out of the car, but her husband, who had been beside her, said that she had not fainted but had simply been tossed from the ride when it whipped around a curve. There were also several issues with the track and trains. For the 1985 season, Six Flags recalled the stand-up trains and restored the original River King Mine Train name.

After the 1987 season, one track was left standing but not operating. It was removed in 1988 and sold, being relocated to Dollywood where opened as Thunder Express in 1989.

The ride's height requirement was increased from to  in 2025.



Black track with unpainted wooden supports.



On October 4, 2025, a 16-year-old employee reportedly fell approximately from the ride's platform.
